coredata.dc:title: Green and simple turn off/on fluorescence sensor for mercury (II), cysteine and histidine
coredata.dc:creator: Tabaraki, Reza
Abdi, Omran
coredata.dc:description: In this study, microwave-assisted synthesized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N-S-CDs) were used Hg2+ ions, cysteine and histidine sensor. The optimum pH was 7 and detection limit and linear range were 8nM and 0.01–50μM, respectively. Fluorescence of N-S-CDs-Hg2+ system was recovered after addition of cysteine and histidine and this effect was used for the determination of these amino acids. The fluorescence recovery of the N-S-CDs–Hg2+ (25μM) system was linearly increased with increasing the concentration of cysteine or histidine from 5 to 50μM and LODs were 0.4μM and 0.6μM for cysteine and histidine, respectively. Simple and green synthesis, solubility in water, no need to any organic solvent and very fast response time were good properties of the new sensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Mercury
Microwave
Cysteine
Histidine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen‑sulfur co-doped carbon dots
Characterization of nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of CDs in aqueous solutions (4.42mg/mL), λex =400nm and λem =490nm. (B) FL spectra at different excitation wavelength.
(A) The typical HRTEM image. (B) FT-IR spectrum of N-S-CDs.
Influence of pH (A) and time (B) on the relative florescence intensity in the presence of 25μM Hg2+.

A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the N-S-CDs in aqueous solution upon addition of various concentrations of Hg2+ (from top to bottom: 0, 0.8, 1, 2, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 35, 40 and 50μM). B) Stern-Volmer plots of N-S-CDs fluorescence-intensity quenching by Hg2+.
Fluorescence responses of N-S-CDs system to different ions and molecules (concentration was 25μM).
Cysteine and histidine bonding to Hg2+.
Fluorescence recovery of the N-S-CDs–Hg2+ system upon the addition of different concentrations of (A) cysteine from 0 to 50μM (B) histidine from 0 to 50μM.
Comparison of the proposed method with the some previously reported fluorescence methods.
Tolerance of diverse ions on the fluorescence intensity of 25μM Hg2+.

row.entry: Sn2+, Sn4+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Sb2+, Ti2+, Ba2+, Li+, W6+, Na+, K+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Li+, Al3+, Acetate, Br−, F−, I−, SO4 2−, CN−, SCN−, l-Arginine, Sucrose, Glucose, Proline, Urea, l-Phenyl Alanine, Glycine, Melamine
Fe3+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Bi3+, Mo6+, La3+, Ag+

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The tolerance limits were determined for a maximum error of 5% in fluorescence intensity.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Hg2+ in the different real samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average of six replicate measurements±standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Green and simple turn off/on fluorescence sensor for mercury (II), cysteine and histidine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Ilam University, Ilam, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, microwave-assisted synthesized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N-S-CDs) were used Hg2+ ions, cysteine and histidine sensor. The optimum pH was 7 and detection limit and linear range were 8nM and 0.01–50μM, respectively. Fluorescence of N-S-CDs-Hg2+ system was recovered after addition of cysteine and histidine and this effect was used for the determination of these amino acids. The fluorescence recovery of the N-S-CDs–Hg2+ (25μM) system was linearly increased with increasing the concentration of cysteine or histidine from 5 to 50μM and LODs were 0.4μM and 0.6μM for cysteine and histidine, respectively. Simple and green synthesis, solubility in water, no need to any organic solvent and very fast response time were good properties of the new sensor.

Turn off/on fluorescence mechanism in Hg2+, cysteine and histidine detection.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury exists as elemental, organic and inorganic forms [1]. Industrial wastes are important sources of the ecosystem contamination and exposure to mercury may damage to brain, kidney and neurological system [2–5]. Several analytical techniques such as atomic absorption spectrometry [6], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [7], X-ray fluorescence spectrometry and gas chromatography–mass spectrometry [8] were reported for Hg2+ determination. Although these methods have low detection limit and wide dynamic range, but they have some difficulties such as expensive instrumentation, problems associated with sample preparation, long analysis time and procedure complexity. Thus, it is essential to develop new methods to overcome these limitations. Different colorimetric or fluorescent sensors were also developed in recent years [9,10]. In the most of the reported colorimetric sensors, organic reagents were used in organic media that had not sufficient selectivity and sensitivity in aqueous media [11,12]. Fluorescence quenching based sensors were also designed for mercury ion [13–15].

In recent years, carbon dots have been considered because of their unique properties such as size-tunable luminescent, broad excitation spectrum, narrow emission spectrum and good capability to functionalization in order to increase selectivity [16]. Among several synthesis methods for the carbon dots preparation, microwave-assisted techniques is simple, low-cost, one-step, environmentally friendly and energy-saving [17–18]. Fluorescence efficiency and selectivity of carbon dots can improve with co-doping of them with nitrogen, sulfur, boron.

The main objectives of this work were synthesis of nitrogen‑sulfur co-doped carbon dots by microwave-assisted method, development of a turn off fluorescent sensor for Hg2+ ions and selective determination of cysteine and histidine by a turn on carbon dots–Hg2+ system.

Chemicals with highest purity available were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). HgCl2 salt was used to prepare stock solution of Hg2+ (10mM). Different ions and molecules were used for interference study. Desired pH values were adjusted by Britton–Robinson universal buffers (0.01M).

Varian Cary 300 Bio, Vertex 70 FT-IR and Varian Cary were used for UV/vis, FT-IR and fluorescence study. High resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM; MC30 Philips) was used for size determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen‑sulfur co-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Microwave-assisted synthesis as an easy and fast method that provides homogeneous heating and uniform size distribution was used for nitrogen‑sulfur co-doped carbon dots preparation. Citric acid (1g) and ammonium thiocyanate (1g) were dissolved in deionized water (10mL). and heated for 5min in a microwave oven at 600W. Brown solid was dissolved in 10mL water, centrifuged to remove impurities and evaporated until dryness at room temperature. Then, it was re-dissolved in distilled water (4.41mg/mL).

In typical analysis, different concentrations of Hg2+ were added to N-S-CDs solution (75μL) and Britton–Robinson universal buffer (3mL, pH=7, 0.01M) and fluorescence was measured (1-cm quartz cuvette, λexcitation =400nm, λemission =480nm, excitation slit width=10nm and emission slit width=5nm).

Real water samples (river, mineral and tap water) were analyzed by new method. The river water samples were filtered by 0.45μm membrane filters and then centrifuged for 15min at 4000rpm. The water samples were spiked by different Hg2+ concentrations and then analyzed with the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: In N-S-CDs absorption spectra (Fig. 1A), there are two absorption peaks (290 and 350nm) that absorption peak at 350nm was related to π→π* transition [19]. When carbon dots were excited at 410nm, strong fluorescence emission was seen at 490nm (Fig. 1A). N-S-CDs fluorescence intensity changed (without significant shift) when excitation wavelength increased from 400 to 460nm (Fig. 1B).

As shown in HRTEM image (Fig. 2A), carbon dots were well dispersed and had diameters about 30nm. FT-IR was used to determine the main functional groups of the N-S-CDs (Fig. 2B). Bands at 3202, 1717 and 2063cm−1 was assigned to OH and NH, carbonyl and azide group vibrations, respectively. These polar groups increase the hydrophilicity of CDs. CN stretching and CS and SN stretching vibrations were observed at region 1192–1406 and 500–700cm−1, respectively [20].

One of the most effective parameter is pH that influences the solubility of metal ions and fluorescence intensity of carbon dots (protonation or deprotonation of the functional groups on the surface in acidic and alkali media) [19,21]. The effect of pH in the range of 2–10 on the fluorescence quenching of CDs in the presence of Hg2+ (25μM) was studied. When pH increased from 2 to 7, the relative fluorescence intensity was increased and had maximum value at pH=7 and then decreased at higher pH (Fig. 3A).

Time effect (1 to 30min) on the relative fluorescence intensity was studied after addition of Hg2+ at room temperature and pH=7. As shown in Fig. 3B, relative fluorescence reached to maximum after 5min. This property is one of the requirements for an effective chemical sensor.

At optimized conditions, Fluorescence intensity of N-S-CDs was gradually decreased with increase in the concentration of Hg2+ ion in the range of 0.01–50μM (Fig. 4A). Formation of a permanent non-luminescent complex between carbon dots and Hg2+ ions (electron donor and the electron acceptor) can lead to quenching of fluorescence [22].

Such quenching effect of Hg2+ can be attributed to electron transfer from excited nitrogen and sulfur atoms in carbon dots to Hg2+. Nitrogen and sulfur atoms are both electron donor and facilitated electron transfer in carbon dots. Radiative recombination of excitons is carbon dots fluorescence source but can be quenched by nonradiative electron-transfer between carbon dots and metal ions [19].

Analytical responses (F/F0 ratio) of the N-S-CDs sensor towards different cations, anions and molecules (concentration of 25μM) were studied. As shown in Fig. 5 , F/F0 ratio had significant decrease after addition of Hg2+ ion, but other ions and molecules had no significant effects. The fluorescence quenching of carbon dots may be result from strong binding between Hg2+ and S atom. The quenching of fluorescence by Hg2+ could be attributed to its ability to facilitate the electron/hole recombination annihilation through an alternate and efficient electron transfer process [29], compared with other metal ions, Hg2+ had a larger ionic radius and polarization, therefore, deformation happened more easily when it interacted with the nitrogen and sulfur atoms [30].

For interference study, the fluorescence intensity was measured in the presence of different ions (Hg2+ concentration was 25μM). No remarkable changes in the fluorescence intensity were observed in the presence of Sn2+, Sn4+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Sb5+, Ti3+, Ba2+, Li+, W6+, Na+, K+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Li+, Al3+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Bi3+, Mo6+, La3+, Ba2+ and Ag+. As shown in Table 2 , this assay is very selective for determination of Hg2+ ions.

In order to investigate of efficiency of the proposed sensor, tap, river and mineral water samples were spiked with different concentration of Hg2+ and analyzed. As shown in Table 3 , recoveries were suitable that showed the accuracy of Hg2+ analysis by this sensor.

Different amino acids were tested for fluorescence recovery of N-S-CDs-Hg2+ system. Some amino acids such as cysteine and histidine are strong Hg2+ chelators because of their specific functional groups [31]. Cysteine (C3H7NO2S) has thiol group and fluorescence recovery was happened in the presence of cysteine and Hg2+ was separated from the surface of carbon dots [32–35]. At pH=7, cysteine exists predominantly in its thiolate form and thus acts as a better coordinating ligand for Hg2+ than other biothiols [36]. Therefore, Cysteine can bind to Hg2+ with higher affinity (the formation constant is about 1042) (Fig. 6 ).

Histidine (C6H9N3O2, an aromatic amino acid) contains α-amino and carboxylic acid groups and a side chain imidazole [31,37]. Fluorescence recovery of the N-S-CDs was also observed with the presence of histidine because of its coordination with Hg2+. It is well known that Hg2+ also has an affinity to certain N-type ligands such as amino acids [38] and the formation constant for histidine bonding to Hg2+ is 1013.25 [39]. The fluorescence recovery of the N-S-CDs–Hg2+ (25μM) system was linearly increased with increasing the concentration of cysteine or histidine from 5 to 50μM (F/F0 =0.0034CCys +0.812, R2 =0.989; F/F0 =0.0032CHis +0.829, R2 =0.990). The LODs were 0.4μM and 0.6μM for cysteine and histidine, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence was recovered with different concentrations of cysteine (5–50μM) or histidine (5–50μM).

A simple and green microwave-assisted method was used to synthesize nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots. These nanoparticles were used in sensitive and selective assay of Hg2+ in real samples. The linear range of N-S-CDs for Hg2+ was 0.01–50μM; while the limit of detection was 8nM. Cysteine and histidine can recover carbon dots fluorescence. Simple and green synthesis, solubility in water, no need to any organic solvent and fast response time were good properties of the new assay.

A highly sensitive and selective competition assay for the detection of cysteine using mercury-specific DNA, Hg2+ and sybr green I
